questions.q: A presheaf of sets on $X$ is, by definition:
choices: a covariant functor $\mathrm{Open}(X) \to \mathsf{Set}$
a contravariant functor $\mathrm{Open}(X) \to \mathsf{Set}$, i.e. a functor $\mathrm{Open}(X)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathsf{Set}$
a sheaf on $X$ satisfying the equalizer condition
a presheaf on the one-point compactification of $X$
questions.explain: Restriction $F(U) \to F(V)$ for $V \subseteq U$ reverses the inclusion arrow $V \hookrightarrow U$, which is exactly contravariance. Equivalently, a presheaf is a functor out of $\mathrm{Open}(X)^{\mathrm{op}}$.

questions.q: For a presheaf $F$ and opens $W \subseteq V \subseteq U$, functoriality demands:
choices: $\rho^U_W = \rho^V_W \circ \rho^U_V$
$\rho^U_W = \rho^U_V \circ \rho^V_W$
$\rho^U_W = \rho^U_V + \rho^V_W$
$\rho^U_W$ depends only on $U$, not on $V$ or $W$
questions.explain: Restricting to $W$ in one shot must equal: restrict to $V$ first, then to $W$. This is the composition law of the contravariant functor, $\rho^U_W = \rho^V_W \circ \rho^U_V$.

questions.q: How many morphisms are there in $\mathrm{Open}(X)$ from $V$ to $U$ when $V \subseteq U$ and $V \ne U$?
questions.explain: $\mathrm{Open}(X)$ is a poset viewed as a category: there is exactly one morphism $V \to U$ when $V \subseteq U$, and none otherwise.

hard.q: Morphisms of presheaves $\varphi\colon F \to G$ are exactly:
choices: maps $\varphi_X\colon F(X) \to G(X)$ on global sections
maps on stalks $\varphi_x\colon F_x \to G_x$ for every $x \in X$
natural transformations of the underlying functors $\mathrm{Open}(X)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathsf{Set}$, i.e. families $\varphi_U\colon F(U) \to G(U)$ commuting with restriction
continuous maps of étalé spaces
hard.hint: Natural transformations commute with every restriction map.
hard.explain: Presheaves are functors, so their morphisms are natural transformations: one map per open, with squares $F(U) \to G(U) \to G(V)$ equal to $F(U) \to F(V) \to G(V)$. Stalk maps are induced, not defining.

hard.q: Which of the following is NOT a presheaf on $\mathbb{R}$ (with its usual topology)?
choices: $U \mapsto C^0(U, \mathbb{R})$, continuous real-valued functions
$U \mapsto \{\text{bounded continuous } f\colon U \to \mathbb{R}\}$
$U \mapsto \mathbb{Z}$ for every nonempty $U$, with all restrictions the identity
$U \mapsto \pi_0(U)$, the set of connected components, with inclusion-induced maps $\pi_0(U) \to \pi_0(V)$ for $V \subseteq U$
hard.hint: Look for an assignment not functorial under restriction.
hard.explain: An inclusion $V \hookrightarrow U$ induces a map $\pi_0(V) \to \pi_0(U)$ (send a component of $V$ to the component of $U$ containing it), going the WRONG way for a presheaf. So $U \mapsto \pi_0(U)$ is naturally a covariant functor on $\mathrm{Open}(X)$, not a presheaf. The other three are presheaves (the bounded one fails to be a sheaf, but is still a presheaf).

hard.q: Let $X$ be a space with $|\mathrm{Open}(X)| = 3$ (e.g. $X = \{a,b\}$ with topology $\{\emptyset, \{a\}, X\}$). A presheaf of sets picks a set $F(U)$ for each open and a restriction for each inclusion. How many non-identity inclusions $V \subsetneq U$ are there in this $\mathrm{Open}(X)$? (These are the non-trivial restrictions you must specify.)
hard.hint: A presheaf of sets is just a functor into Set.
hard.explain: The three opens are $\emptyset \subsetneq \{a\} \subsetneq X$. Strict inclusions: $\emptyset \subsetneq \{a\}$, $\emptyset \subsetneq X$, and $\{a\} \subsetneq X$ — three in total.

questions.q: A presheaf $F$ on $X$ is a sheaf iff, for every open $U \subseteq X$ and every open cover $U = \bigcup_i U_i$:
choices: the restriction maps $F(U)\to F(U_i)$ are all surjective
the diagram $F(U)\to \prod_i F(U_i)\rightrightarrows \prod_{i,j} F(U_i\cap U_j)$ is an equalizer
$F(U) = \bigoplus_i F(U_i)$
the stalks $F_x$ all agree for $x\in U$
questions.explain: The sheaf axiom packages locality (injectivity on $F(U)\to\prod F(U_i)$) and gluing (compatible families lift) as the equalizer condition.

questions.q: Let $B$ be the presheaf on $\mathbb{R}$ with $B(U) = \{$bounded continuous $f\colon U\to\mathbb{R}\}$. Which sheaf axiom does $B$ fail?
choices: locality (separation)
gluing (existence)
functoriality of restriction
$B(\emptyset)$ is not terminal
questions.explain: Bounded functions glue to an unbounded function: on $\mathbb{R} = \bigcup_n (-n,n)$ the compatible family $f_n(x)=x$ has no bounded global lift, so gluing fails.

questions.q: For a sheaf of abelian groups $F$, what is $|F(\emptyset)|$? (Enter the size of the underlying set.)
questions.explain: The empty cover of $\emptyset$ forces $F(\emptyset)$ to be the terminal object of the target category — for abelian groups, the zero group $\{0\}$, which has $1$ element.

hard.q: Consider the constant presheaf $\underline{\mathbb{Z}}^{\mathrm{pre}}$ on a disconnected space $X = U_1 \sqcup U_2$ assigning $\mathbb{Z}$ to every non-empty open. Its sheafification $\underline{\mathbb{Z}}$ satisfies $\underline{\mathbb{Z}}(X) \cong$ :
choices: $\mathbb{Z}$
$\mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z}$
$\mathbb{Z}[x]$
hard.hint: Disconnected opens fail gluing for the constant presheaf.
hard.explain: On $X = U_1 \sqcup U_2$ the sheaf condition forces $F(X) \cong F(U_1)\times F(U_2)$ because $\{U_1,U_2\}$ is a cover with $U_1\cap U_2=\emptyset$. The constant presheaf assigned $\mathbb{Z}$ to $X$, but its sheafification assigns $\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{Z}$, showing constant presheaf $\neq$ constant sheaf on disconnected spaces.

hard.q: Which is the correct order of steps in verifying the equalizer condition for the sheaf $\mathcal{O}_X$ of holomorphic functions on a Riemann surface?
choices: Gluing → Locality → Functoriality
Functoriality → Gluing → Locality
Locality (injectivity of $e$) first — if $f|_{U_i}=g|_{U_i}$ for all $i$ then $f=g$ — then Gluing (surjectivity onto the equalizer) — a compatible family $(f_i)$ extends to $f\in\mathcal{O}(U)$
Gluing first, then Locality, then the empty-set check
hard.hint: Verify equalizer: identity of sections on overlaps, then glue.
hard.explain: The standard verification separates into two parts matching the two parts of the equalizer: first prove $e:F(U)\to\prod F(U_i)$ is injective (locality/separation), then prove every element of the equalizer is in the image (gluing/descent).

hard.q: Let $F$ be the skyscraper sheaf at $x\in X$ with stalk $\mathbb{Z}/5\mathbb{Z}$. For any open $U$ not containing $x$, $|F(U)|$ equals what?
hard.hint: Skyscraper sheaf assigns the singleton to any open missing $x$.
hard.explain: A skyscraper sheaf assigns the trivial group (of size $1$) to every open set not containing the base point $x$, and the stalk group $\mathbb{Z}/5\mathbb{Z}$ to every open containing $x$.

questions.q: The stalk $F_x$ of a sheaf at $x\in X$ is defined as:
choices: $\bigoplus_{x\in U} F(U)$
$\varinjlim_{x\in U} F(U)$, filtered over open neighborhoods of $x$
$\varprojlim_{x\in U} F(U)$
questions.explain: A germ at $x$ is an equivalence class $[(U,s)]$ of a section on some open neighborhood, modulo agreement on smaller neighborhoods — exactly the filtered colimit $\varinjlim_{x\in U} F(U)$.

questions.q: For a sheaf $F$ and sections $s,t\in F(U)$ with $s_x = t_x$ for every $x\in U$, which conclusion holds?
choices: $s = t$
$s$ and $t$ need not agree, only their germs do
$s = t$ only if $U$ is connected
$s = t$ only if $F$ is a sheaf of abelian groups
questions.explain: Agreement on all stalks produces an open cover on which $s$ and $t$ coincide; the sheaf's locality axiom then forces $s = t$ on $U$.

questions.q: On a scheme $X = \mathrm{Spec}\,R$, the stalk of $\mathcal{O}_X$ at the prime $\mathfrak{p}$ is $R_{\mathfrak{p}}$. For $R = \mathbb{Z}$ and $\mathfrak{p} = (5)$, what is the residue field $R_{\mathfrak{p}}/\mathfrak{p}R_{\mathfrak{p}}$'s characteristic?
questions.explain: $\mathbb{Z}_{(5)}/5\mathbb{Z}_{(5)} = \mathbb{F}_5$, the residue field at the prime $(5)$, which has characteristic $5$.

hard.q: A morphism $\varphi:F\to G$ of sheaves on $X$ is an isomorphism if and only if:
choices: $\varphi(U):F(U)\to G(U)$ is an isomorphism for every open $U$
$\varphi$ is injective and surjective as a natural transformation on opens
the induced map on stalks $\varphi_x:F_x\to G_x$ is an isomorphism for every $x\in X$
$\varphi(X):F(X)\to G(X)$ is an isomorphism
hard.hint: Stalk-wise isomorphism is equivalent to being a sheaf iso.
hard.explain: For sheaves, a morphism is an isomorphism iff it is an isomorphism on all stalks. This fails for presheaves — the key point is that sheafification preserves stalks, so stalk-wise data is the correct local invariant.

hard.q: For $\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{C},0}$ the stalk of holomorphic functions at $0\in\mathbb{C}$, the function $f(z)=z^3+2z$ has germ $[(U,f)]$ at $0$. What is the value of this germ evaluated at $z=0$, i.e., $f(0)$?
hard.hint: The germ of $z^3 + 2z$ at $0$ has value $0$.
hard.explain: $f(0) = 0^3 + 2\cdot 0 = 0$. The germ encodes all local data of $f$ near $0$, including $f(0)=0$.

hard.q: The étalé space $\acute{E}t(F)$ of a sheaf $F$ is constructed so that sections of $F$ over $U$ correspond bijectively to:
choices: all continuous maps $U\to\acute{E}t(F)$
continuous sections $s:U\to\acute{E}t(F)$ of the projection $\pi:\acute{E}t(F)\to X$
open subsets of $\acute{E}t(F)$ lying over $U$
locally constant maps $U\to\bigsqcup_{x\in U}F_x$
hard.hint: Etale space sections are continuous local sections over $U$.
hard.explain: The étalé (or étale) space is $\acute{E}t(F)=\bigsqcup_{x\in X}F_x$ topologized so that $F(U)\cong\{$continuous sections of $\pi$ over $U\}$. This is the sheaf-of-sections construction and underpins the equivalence between sheaves and étalé bundles.

sheaf-morphisms-stalks.title: Morphisms of sheaves and stalk criteria

questions.q: For a morphism $\varphi\colon F \to G$ of sheaves on $X$, which stalk criterion is correct?
choices: $\varphi$ is injective $\iff$ $\varphi_U\colon F(U) \to G(U)$ is injective for every open $U$, which is equivalent to $\varphi_x\colon F_x \to G_x$ being injective for every $x$
$\varphi$ is surjective $\iff$ $\varphi_U\colon F(U) \to G(U)$ is surjective for every open $U$
$\varphi$ is a monomorphism $\iff$ there exists some open $U$ with $\varphi_U$ injective
stalk maps carry no information about $\varphi$
questions.explain: Monomorphisms in $\mathsf{Sh}(X)$ coincide with sectionwise injective, equivalently stalkwise injective. Epimorphisms are stalkwise surjective but NOT sectionwise surjective in general — that asymmetry is the seed of sheaf cohomology.

questions.q: On $X = \mathbb{C}^\times$, consider $\exp\colon \mathcal{O}_X \to \mathcal{O}_X^\times$ sending $f \mapsto e^f$. This map is:
choices: stalkwise surjective but not globally surjective; the global section $z \in \mathcal{O}_X^\times(X)$ is not in the image of $\exp_X$
stalkwise surjective and globally surjective
neither stalkwise nor globally surjective
globally surjective but not stalkwise surjective
questions.explain: Locally on a small disc a holomorphic logarithm exists, so $\exp_x$ is surjective on every stalk. But no global holomorphic logarithm of $z$ exists on $\mathbb{C}^\times$ (the branch cut obstructs it), so $z \not\in \exp(\mathcal{O}(\mathbb{C}^\times))$.

questions.q: A short exact sequence $0 \to F' \to F \to F'' \to 0$ of sheaves of abelian groups on $X$ yields a long exact sequence in sheaf cohomology. The cohomology degree where the first new obstruction to global surjectivity appears is $H^i(X, F')$ for $i = ?$
questions.explain: Applying $\Gamma(X,-)$ gives $0 \to \Gamma F' \to \Gamma F \to \Gamma F'' \to H^1(X, F') \to \cdots$. The connecting map lands in $H^1$, which measures exactly the failure of $\Gamma F \to \Gamma F''$ to be surjective.

hard.q: For a morphism $\varphi\colon F \to G$ of sheaves of abelian groups, how is the image sheaf $\mathrm{im}\,\varphi$ defined, and why?
choices: as the presheaf $U \mapsto \varphi_U(F(U)) \subseteq G(U)$, because presheaf images are already sheaves
as the sheafification of $U \mapsto \varphi_U(F(U))$, because the presheaf image need not satisfy gluing — sections that are locally in the image may not come from a global preimage
as the kernel of the cokernel map $G \to \mathrm{coker}\,\varphi$, which by definition is the presheaf image
as $\ker(\varphi)^\perp$ inside $G$
hard.hint: Sheafify the naive presheaf image to define the image sheaf.
hard.explain: The presheaf image $U \mapsto \varphi_U F(U)$ captures 'sections of $G$ actually coming from $F$ on $U$', but a section of $G$ that is locally of this form need not lift to a global preimage — exactly the exponential example. Sheafifying fixes this, and on stalks one still has $(\mathrm{im}\,\varphi)_x = \mathrm{im}(\varphi_x)$.

hard.q: Let $\varphi\colon F \to G$ be a morphism of sheaves of sets with $\varphi_x\colon F_x \to G_x$ a bijection for every $x \in X$. Then:
choices: $\varphi$ is an isomorphism of sheaves
$\varphi_U\colon F(U) \to G(U)$ is a bijection for every $U$, but $\varphi$ need not be an iso in $\mathsf{Sh}(X)$
only $\varphi_X$ is a bijection; smaller opens may fail
nothing can be concluded without global information
hard.hint: Stalkwise bijection may not force global bijection without exactness.
hard.explain: For sheaves, stalkwise iso $\iff$ iso. Sectionwise bijectivity is actually a consequence: stalkwise injective gives sectionwise injective (using locality), and stalkwise surjective + locality + gluing of $F$ gives sectionwise surjective. Together: $\varphi_U$ is a bijection for every $U$, hence $\varphi$ is an iso in $\mathsf{Sh}(X)$.

hard.q: Let $X = \mathbb{C}^\times$. The cokernel sheaf of $\exp\colon \mathcal{O}_X \to \mathcal{O}_X^\times$ (stalkwise surjective) is the zero sheaf, yet the presheaf cokernel $U \mapsto \mathcal{O}^\times(U)/\exp \mathcal{O}(U)$ evaluated at $U = X$ is a nontrivial group isomorphic to $H^1(\mathbb{C}^\times, \underline{2\pi i\mathbb{Z}})$. What is the rank of this $\mathbb{Z}$-module?
hard.hint: Cokernel on $\mathbb{C}^\times$ is trivial as a sheaf but non-trivial as a presheaf.
hard.explain: $H^1(\mathbb{C}^\times, \underline{\mathbb{Z}}) \cong \mathbb{Z}$ (generated by the winding-number cocycle), so the $\mathbb{Z}$-rank is $1$. This is why the presheaf cokernel disagrees with the sheaf cokernel: sheafification wipes out the obstruction, which reappears as $H^1$.

questions.q: The sheafification functor $(-)^\#\colon \mathsf{PSh}(X) \to \mathsf{Sh}(X)$ is:
choices: right adjoint to the inclusion $\iota\colon \mathsf{Sh}(X) \hookrightarrow \mathsf{PSh}(X)$
left adjoint to the inclusion $\iota\colon \mathsf{Sh}(X) \hookrightarrow \mathsf{PSh}(X)$, so $\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathsf{Sh}}(F^\#, G) \cong \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathsf{PSh}}(F, \iota G)$
an equivalence of categories $\mathsf{PSh}(X) \simeq \mathsf{Sh}(X)$
not a functor in general
questions.explain: Sheafification is the left adjoint to $\iota$. The universal property: any presheaf map $F \to \iota G$ into a sheaf factors uniquely through the unit $\eta\colon F \to F^\#$.

questions.q: Which invariant of a presheaf $F$ is preserved by sheafification?
choices: the global sections $F(X)$
the sections $F(U)$ for every open $U$
the stalks $F_x$ at every point
nothing — sheafification changes everything
questions.explain: The unit $\eta\colon F \to F^\#$ induces an isomorphism $F_x \xrightarrow{\sim} (F^\#)_x$ on every stalk. Global and sectional data CAN change — e.g. the constant presheaf on a disconnected space has $F(X) = S$ but $F^\#(X) = S^{\#(\mathrm{components})}$.

questions.q: Let $X = U_1 \sqcup U_2 \sqcup U_3$ be a disjoint union of three nonempty opens, and let $F = \underline{\mathbb{Z}/2}^{\mathrm{pre}}$ be the constant presheaf with value $\mathbb{Z}/2$. How many elements does the sheafified global-sections set $F^\#(X)$ have?
questions.explain: The constant sheaf $\underline{\mathbb{Z}/2}$ assigns to $X$ the locally-constant functions $X \to \mathbb{Z}/2$. With three components, this is $(\mathbb{Z}/2)^3$, which has $2^3 = 8$ elements. The unsheafified presheaf $F(X)$ has only $2$ elements, so sheafification has multiplied the global sections from $2$ to $8$.

hard.q: A reflective subcategory $\mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathcal{D}$ is one where the inclusion has a left adjoint $L\colon \mathcal{D} \to \mathcal{C}$. Why is $\mathsf{Sh}(X) \subseteq \mathsf{PSh}(X)$ with reflector $(-)^\#$ a good thing for computing in sheaves?
choices: colimits in $\mathsf{Sh}(X)$ are computed by taking the presheaf colimit and sheafifying — one can work pointwise and fix up gluing only at the end
limits in $\mathsf{Sh}(X)$ require sheafifying
every sheaf morphism extends non-uniquely to a presheaf morphism
sheaves are closed under arbitrary set-theoretic operations in presheaves
hard.hint: The reflection unit provides the universal sheafification map.
hard.explain: Left adjoints preserve colimits. So colimits in $\mathsf{Sh}(X)$ are the reflection of presheaf colimits: compute pointwise, then sheafify. Limits, by contrast, are computed pointwise directly and do not need sheafification — the inclusion $\iota$ preserves limits as a right adjoint.

hard.q: Sheafification of abelian presheaves $(-)^\#\colon \mathsf{PSh}(X, \mathsf{Ab}) \to \mathsf{Sh}(X, \mathsf{Ab})$ is exact. Which combination of facts is essential to this exactness?
choices: sheafification preserves stalks, and a sequence of sheaves of abelian groups is exact iff exact on every stalk
sheafification is a right adjoint
the sheaf condition is a finite-limit condition, and every functor preserves finite limits
exactness in $\mathsf{Sh}(X, \mathsf{Ab})$ means sectionwise exactness
hard.hint: Exactness comes from stalks being preserved.
hard.explain: Two ingredients: (1) sheafification preserves stalks (it's the universal gluing fix that leaves germs alone), (2) exactness of sheaves of abelian groups is stalk-local. Combined: $(F^\#)_x = F_x$, so stalk-exact in presheaves $\implies$ stalk-exact in sheaves $\iff$ exact in $\mathsf{Sh}(X, \mathsf{Ab})$.

hard.q: Let $F$ be any presheaf on a space $X$. The 'plus construction' $F^+$ applied once yields a separated presheaf; applied twice yields a sheaf. How many applications of $(-)^+$ are required in general to land in $\mathsf{Sh}(X)$?
hard.hint: First plus-construction separates; second makes gluing work.
hard.explain: Grothendieck's theorem: $F^{++} = F^\#$ is always a sheaf, so two applications suffice. One application is not enough: $F^+$ is separated (locality holds) but may still fail gluing.

questions.q: A ringed space is a pair $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ where:
choices: $X$ is a ring and $\mathcal{O}_X$ is its spectrum
$X$ is a topological space and $\mathcal{O}_X$ is a sheaf of (commutative, unital) rings on $X$
$X$ is a set and $\mathcal{O}_X$ is a ring acting on it
$X$ is a topological space and $\mathcal{O}_X$ is just a ring — the same ring on every open
questions.explain: A ringed space packages 'geometry' ($X$, a topological space) with 'functions' ($\mathcal{O}_X$, a sheaf of rings). Examples: $(M, C^\infty_M)$ for a smooth manifold; $(\mathrm{Spec}\,R, \widetilde{R})$ for an affine scheme.

questions.q: A ringed space $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ is called LOCALLY ringed when:
choices: the sheaf $\mathcal{O}_X$ is defined only locally on an open cover
every stalk $\mathcal{O}_{X,x}$ is a local ring (has a unique maximal ideal)
$X$ is locally compact
$\mathcal{O}_X(U)$ is a local ring for every open $U$
questions.explain: Local ringedness is the stalk-level condition: every $\mathcal{O}_{X,x}$ is a local ring. The maximal ideal is 'functions vanishing at $x$', and the residue field $\kappa(x) = \mathcal{O}_{X,x}/\mathfrak{m}_x$ is the field of values at $x$. Smooth manifolds, complex manifolds, and schemes are all locally ringed; general ringed spaces need not be.

questions.q: For a smooth manifold $M$, the sheaf of sections of the trivial rank-$5$ bundle $M \times \mathbb{R}^5 \to M$ is a locally free $C^\infty_M$-module of rank equal to what integer?
questions.explain: The locally-free-sheaf / vector-bundle dictionary: rank of the bundle = rank of the module. A rank-$n$ bundle corresponds to a locally free $\mathcal{O}_M$-module of rank $n$; the trivial rank-$5$ bundle gives $(C^\infty_M)^5$, a free (hence locally free) module of rank $5$.

hard.q: A morphism of locally ringed spaces $(f, f^\#)\colon (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$ includes a data piece beyond a continuous map + sheaf-of-rings map. What is the extra condition?
choices: the induced stalk map $f^\#_x\colon \mathcal{O}_{Y, f(x)} \to \mathcal{O}_{X, x}$ is a local ring homomorphism: it sends the maximal ideal of $\mathcal{O}_{Y, f(x)}$ INTO the maximal ideal of $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$
$f$ is a homeomorphism
$f^\#$ is an isomorphism of sheaves of rings
every stalk of $\mathcal{O}_X$ is a field
hard.hint: Local ring maps respect maximal ideals.
hard.explain: Without the local-ring condition on stalks, one gets 'ring-valued' morphisms that don't correspond to anything geometric — e.g. between schemes one could otherwise construct a map $\mathrm{Spec}\,k(t) \to \mathrm{Spec}\,k[t]_{(t)}$ that inverts the inclusion, which is not a scheme morphism. Locality of the stalk map is exactly what pins down 'the point goes to the point it should.'

hard.q: For an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{F}$ on a ringed space, which statement about tensor product is correct?
choices: $(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})(U) = \mathcal{F}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{G}(U)$ for every open $U$, with no sheafification needed

$\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}$ is defined as the sheafification of $U \mapsto \mathcal{F}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{G}(U)$, and its stalks are $\mathcal{F}_x \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{X,x}} \mathcal{G}_x$
internal $\mathcal{H}\!om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})$ requires sheafification, but $\otimes$ does not
$\otimes$ is computed stalkwise and glued — no formula on open sets
hard.hint: Tensor with $\mathcal{O}_X$ sheafifies locally.
hard.explain: The presheaf $U \mapsto \mathcal{F}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{G}(U)$ generally fails the gluing axiom, so tensor product of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules is defined by sheafifying. By contrast, $\mathcal{H}\!om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})$ is already a sheaf (hom satisfies gluing because morphisms glue).

hard.q: On $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}$ with structure sheaf $\mathcal{O}_X$, the stalk at the prime $(0)$ is $\mathbb{Q}$. What is the residue field $\kappa((0)) = \mathcal{O}_{X,(0)}/\mathfrak{m}_{(0)}$? (Enter $0$ if it is zero, or the characteristic of the residue field otherwise.)
hard.hint: At the generic point the structure sheaf localizes to the field of fractions.
hard.explain: $\mathbb{Q}$ is already a field, so its maximal ideal is $(0)$ and the residue field is $\mathbb{Q}/(0) = \mathbb{Q}$, which has characteristic $0$. At closed points $(p)$ the residue field is $\mathbb{F}_p$; at the generic point $(0)$ the residue field is $\mathbb{Q}$.

questions.q: The sheafification $F^+$ of a presheaf $F$ is characterized by:
choices: $F^+(U) = F(U)$ for all $U$
$F^+$ is the initial sheaf receiving a map from $F$ (universal property): any $F\to G$ with $G$ a sheaf factors uniquely through $F\to F^+$
$F^+(U) = \prod_{x\in U} F_x$
$F^+ = F$ whenever $F$ is separated
questions.explain: Sheafification is left-adjoint to the inclusion $\mathsf{Sh}(X)\hookrightarrow\mathsf{PSh}(X)$: it enforces gluing while preserving stalks $(F^+)_x = F_x$.

questions.q: On $X = \mathrm{Spec}\,R$, the functor $M\mapsto \widetilde{M}$ taking an $R$-module to its associated quasi-coherent sheaf:
choices: is fully faithful but not essentially surjective
is an equivalence $R\text{-}\mathsf{Mod} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathsf{QCoh}(\mathrm{Spec}\,R)$
is only defined for Noetherian $R$
always lands in coherent sheaves
questions.explain: The key theorem: $M\mapsto\widetilde{M}$ is an equivalence of categories with inverse $\mathcal{F}\mapsto\Gamma(X,\mathcal{F})$. This reduces quasi-coherent sheaf theory on an affine scheme to module theory.

questions.q: For $R=\mathbb{Z}$, $M = \mathbb{Z}/6$, and $f = 2$, the section $\widetilde{M}(D(2)) = M_2 = M[\tfrac{1}{2}]$ has how many elements?
questions.explain: Inverting $2$ in $\mathbb{Z}/6$ kills the $2$-part: $(\mathbb{Z}/6)[1/2] = \mathbb{Z}/3$, which has $3$ elements.

hard.q: Which is the correct sequence of steps in the two-step construction of sheafification $F\mapsto F^+$?
choices: First sheafify, then separate
First apply $F\mapsto F^+$ to get a separated presheaf, then apply $(-)^+$ again to get a sheaf; the result $(F^+)^+$ is the sheafification
Apply the plus-construction once; the result is already a sheaf by the universal property
Take the colimit over all refinements of covers directly
hard.hint: Start with the plus-construction twice.
hard.explain: The plus-construction $F^+$ yields a separated presheaf from any presheaf. Applying it a second time to the separated presheaf yields the sheaf $(F^+)^+$. Grothendieck showed two applications always suffice.

hard.q: Let $R = k[x,y]/(xy)$ and $M = R/(x)\cong k[y]$. The localization $M_x = M[1/x]$ has how many elements when $k = \mathbb{F}_2$ and we count elements of $M_x$ that are annihilated by $x$? (Hint: $xM=0$.)
hard.hint: Since $xy = 0$ forces $x$ invertible to kill $y$, the localization collapses.
hard.explain: Since $x$ annihilates every element of $M = k[y]$ (as $x\cdot k[y]=0$ in $R/(x)$), inverting $x$ forces $M_x = 0$. So every element of $M_x$ is $0$, and $0$ elements are annihilated by $x$ in a trivial group sense — $M_x$ itself has size $1$ (the zero module), so there are $0$ non-zero elements annihilated by $x$.

hard.q: A coherent sheaf on a Noetherian scheme $X$ is a quasi-coherent sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ that is additionally:
choices: globally generated by finitely many sections
locally finitely presented: on each affine $\mathrm{Spec}\,R_i$ the corresponding module $M_i$ is finitely generated over $R_i$
a direct sum of line bundles
flat over $\mathcal{O}_X$
hard.hint: Coherent means finitely generated with finitely presented kernel on opens.
hard.explain: Coherence requires local finite generation (and, for non-Noetherian rings, also that the kernel of any map from a free finite-rank sheaf is finitely generated). On a Noetherian scheme, local finite generation suffices.
